Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I'd dedicate about three hours a day to studying, but I rarely followed a strict schedule. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams, mind maps, and color-coded notes from the course materials. The gamified tasks actually helped me structure things a bit better because they gave me clear milestones to hit. I'd often study in intense bursts rather than spreading things evenly throughout the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was coming up. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming hard before a deadline or exam. My extracurricular activities took up significant time, so I had to be flexible. When anxiety kicked in about upcoming assessments, I'd suddenly become very disciplined for a few days. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but somehow I made it work.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a new gamified challenge or the panic of realizing an assignment was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements or updates I'd missed. The gamification elements actually motivated me to log in more than I would have otherwise—earning points and badges felt satisfying. If I'm being honest, I probably should have logged in more regularly, but without internet at home, it was always a deliberate trip to campus.
Q4: The gamified tasks definitely helped make the LMS feel more like something I wanted to check rather than something I had to check. When I started seeing my progress on the leaderboards and collecting badges, it became almost addictive for a while. But I wouldn't say it ever became a true daily habit because of my internet situation. I'd batch my LMS activities when I was on campus with reliable wifi.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me as a visual learner—I watched them multiple times, probably why my watch time is over two hours. The discussion forums were also valuable because seeing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The gamified elements made completing tasks feel rewarding, which kept me motivated even when anxiety was high. I also appreciated any materials with diagrams or visual representations of complex ideas.
Q6: I tended to skip the purely text-based readings when I could find video alternatives or visual summaries. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant after I'd already grasped the concept through other means. I also ignored some of the peer interaction features when my anxiety was particularly bad—sometimes engaging with others felt overwhelming. If something didn't seem directly connected to assessments, I'd often deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it either directly helped me prepare for exams or gave me that satisfying sense of progress through the gamification system. Visual content that I could actually see and process quickly always felt more valuable than dense text. If I could complete something and immediately see the impact on my understanding or my course standing, that made it feel worthwhile. The badges and points system honestly made even mundane tasks feel more engaging.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which actually made them more appealing sometimes. With required work, there was always this underlying anxiety about getting it right, but optional stuff I could approach more casually. The gamified optional tasks were particularly engaging because they felt like bonuses I was earning. Required activities carried more weight mentally, which sometimes made me procrastinate on them more.
Q9: Absolutely—I did a huge amount of studying offline that never showed up in the system. Since I don't have reliable internet at home, I'd download everything I could when on campus and then study from those files for hours. I created extensive handwritten notes and visual diagrams that were my primary study tools. I also used textbooks and YouTube videos for additional explanations of difficult concepts.
Q10: Most of my actual deep learning happened when I was sitting with my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams at home, completely offline. I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper that the LMS would never capture. Sometimes I'd watch a lecture video once on the LMS but then replay my downloaded version multiple times. My exam scores reflect knowledge I built largely through offline work that left no digital trace.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach because of my internet situation. I'd download lecture videos, PDFs, and any other materials I could access, then study them extensively offline. A single login might represent hours of subsequent offline engagement. I became really efficient at grabbing everything I needed during my campus visits so I could continue learning at home without connectivity.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt impossible. When multiple deadlines converged with my extracurricular commitments, I'd feel paralyzed and avoid the LMS entirely for days. Sometimes after checking my grades or feedback, if it wasn't what I hoped for, I'd need time to recover before diving back in. The low mood after some interventions made it hard to maintain consistent engagement.
Q13: My extracurricular activities definitely competed for my time and mental energy. When I was feeling particularly anxious, even logging into the LMS felt like a big step. Fatigue from trying to balance everything meant I sometimes accessed materials but couldn't really absorb them. The lack of home internet added an extra layer of planning and stress to everything I did for the course.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video or scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere because of anxiety. Sometimes I'd log in and just stare at the screen, feeling overwhelmed by everything I needed to do. I'd click through things to feel like I was being productive without actually processing any of it. Those sessions technically counted as engagement but they were basically empty.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important—my completion rate shows that clearly. The anxiety made starting things feel incredibly daunting, so I'd avoid them until the pressure became unbearable. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace, but tomorrow would bring its own challenges. The gamified tasks were easier to start because they felt less high-stakes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator, which isn't great but it's honest. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, then relative quiet afterward. The anxiety around deadlines was terrible, but it was also the push I needed to actually complete things. Without clear deadlines, I'd often let things drift indefinitely.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me because of my anxiety. Positive feedback would boost my confidence and make me more likely to engage, but negative feedback could send me into an avoidance spiral. When I got good scores on assignments or exams, it validated my approach and motivated me to continue. Sometimes I'd delay checking feedback because I was afraid of what it might say.
Q18: The gamification elements really helped give me structure and motivation that I might not have had otherwise. Having clear milestones and visible progress made the course feel more manageable despite my sporadic attendance. The course structure worked well for someone like me who does intense bursts of work rather than steady engagement. I appreciated that I could still succeed even without being physically present all the time.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks with immediate rewards. Visual materials were easier for me to engage with than text-heavy content. What made things harder was when too many things were due at once or when the path forward wasn't clear. The lack of flexibility in some deadlines didn't accommodate my unpredictable schedule well.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality submissions. High anxiety can make you engage with content repeatedly without feeling confident enough to submit work. Maybe they're perfectionists who keep revising but never feel ready to turn things in. Or they could be like me—doing a lot of offline work that doesn't translate into visible LMS submissions.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well—my attendance and task completion rates are low, but my exam and assignment scores are high. I think it shows that deep learning can happen outside the LMS, especially for students who study offline. Some of us are just efficient at extracting what we need from limited engagement and then processing it independently. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with my notes and diagrams.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone accesses materials and how often they log in. They capture video watch times and discussion participation pretty accurately. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and the emotional state of the student. They can't tell if someone is anxiously clicking through materials or deeply focused and learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively creating connections between concepts and thinking critically about what I'm learning. For me, engagement often looks like sitting with my hand-drawn diagrams, testing myself, and genuinely wanting to master the content. It's a mental and emotional investment that goes way beyond just showing up or logging in.
Q24: The LMS can't show the hours I spent studying offline without internet access, creating visual notes and diagrams. It doesn't capture my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it took just to log in some days. My resilience in pushing through despite high anxiety and competing demands is invisible in the data. The gamification genuinely helped me stay motivated, but the depth of that psychological impact isn't measurable in clicks and timestamps.
